coredata.dc:title: Enhancing the thermal stability of activated carbon in VOCs adsorption by incorporation of silicon-doped CQDs
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Hongwei
Han, Liang
Li, Qing
Liu, Xiaoyun
Huang, Xiaoyu
Jia, Luyu
Huang, Zhaoxiong
Zhang, Jinfeng
coredata.dc:description: As a widely utilized adsorbent for volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from industrial waste gases, activated carbon (AC) poses a risk of fire due to its tendency of spontaneous combustion under oxidizing conditions. The introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into AC via immersion method can improve its thermal stability thus reducing the fire risk. Meanwhile, it can further enhance its VOCs adsorption performance. The Si-doped CQDs modified AC was synthesized and characterized using TEM, FT-IR, and XRD. After modification, a significant presence of crystalline structures and compact structures were observed on the surface of AC, resulting in increased intermolecular forces and suppressed thermal decomposition of AC. The natural tendency and thermal safety of modified AC were studied by thermal analysis. The spontaneous combustion results showed that after toluene adsorption, the autoignition temperature of Si-doped CQDs modified AC was 59.7 ℃, much higher than that of AC. The activation energy increased from 31.90 kJ·mol−1 to 69.97 kJ·mol−1. The relative fire safety factor of Si-doped CQDs modified AC was 1.3, superior to unmodified AC, significantly reducing the thermal safety risk. The thermal investigation during the toluene adsorption of AC were studied by μRC. The study showed that the heat released by the modified AC at different temperatures was significantly lower than the unmodified AC. The Si-doped CQDs modified AC effectively increased the thermal stability of the AC and significantly reduced the propensity for fire hazard of VOCs adsorption by AC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Activated carbon
Carbon quantum dots
Thermal stability
Volatile organic compounds
Spontaneous combustion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Spontaneous combustion properties of AC and AC/Si-CQDs
Thermal analysis of AC and AC/Si-CQDs
The fire resistance of AC/Si-CQDs in toluene adsorption
Mechanism of interaction between Si-CQDs and AC

(a) XPS full spectrum of Si-CQDs; (b) C1s;(c) O1s;(d) Si2p.
(a b) The different magnification of AC/Si-CQDs TEM image; (c)SEM image of AC; (d) SEM image of AC/Si-CQDs.
FTIR spectrum of AC and AC/Si-CQDs.
TG-DTG curves of AC and AC/Si-CQDs in air atmosphere.
Variation trend of spontaneous ignition temperature of AC with Si-CQDs content.

Calculation results of apparent activation energy of materials.
The adsorption capacity of AC and AC/Si-CQDs to toluene.
Spontaneous ignition point of AC and AC/Si-CQDs after adsorption of toluene.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: LA/SiO2 is a lauric acid/silica composite phase change material. S4SQ is a sesquisiloxane derivative.

head.ce:title: Enhancing the thermal stability of activated carbon in VOCs adsorption by incorporation of silicon-doped CQDs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering. Hebei University of Science and Technology, Shijiazhuang, Hebei 050018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering. Hebei University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering. Hebei University of Science and Technology, Shijiazhuang, Hebei, 050018, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a widely utilized adsorbent for volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from industrial waste gases, activated carbon (AC) poses a risk of fire due to its tendency of spontaneous combustion under oxidizing conditions. The introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into AC via immersion method can improve its thermal stability thus reducing the fire risk. Meanwhile, it can further enhance its VOCs adsorption performance. The Si-doped CQDs modified AC was synthesized and characterized using TEM, FT-IR, and XRD. After modification, a significant presence of crystalline structures and compact structures were observed on the surface of AC, resulting in increased intermolecular forces and suppressed thermal decomposition of AC. The natural tendency and thermal safety of modified AC were studied by thermal analysis. The spontaneous combustion results showed that after toluene adsorption, the autoignition temperature of Si-doped CQDs modified AC was 59.7 ℃, much higher than that of AC. The activation energy increased from 31.90 kJ·mol−1 to 69.97 kJ·mol−1. The relative fire safety factor of Si-doped CQDs modified AC was 1.3, superior to unmodified AC, significantly reducing the thermal safety risk. The thermal investigation during the toluene adsorption of AC were studied by μRC. The study showed that the heat released by the modified AC at different temperatures was significantly lower than the unmodified AC. The Si-doped CQDs modified AC effectively increased the thermal stability of the AC and significantly reduced the propensity for fire hazard of VOCs adsorption by AC.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that supports the findings of this study are available in the supplementary material of this article.

ce:section.ce:para: Activated carbon (AC), with its rich pore structure and excellent adsorption performance, is an ideal adsorbent for volatile organic compounds (VOCs) [1,2]. This recyclable adsorbent performs well in dealing with high-flow, low-concentration exhaust gases, hence it has been widely used [3–5]. However, the combustibility and fire hazard of AC in VOCs adsorption has not been paid enough attention although it has led to lots of fire accidents. AC itself has a high calorific value. When the oxygen in the air is sufficient to support the reaction between AC and oxygen, the heat generated by the low-temperature oxidation of AC cannot be fully dissipated through conduction or convection [6]. The accumulated heat that cannot be released may lead to heat storage oxidation self-ignition [7,8]. On the other hand, most of the VOCs adsorbed in the gas phase adsorption process are flammable, explosive, and volatile compounds. The oxidation reaction of AC with oxygen releases heat, raising the system temperature, and is thus prone to combustion accidents [9,10]. During transportation, storage, and use, AC fire accidents are also common, such as AC catching fire due to heat accumulation, dust explosions during the production of AC, and fires in the AC during solvent recovery. The flammability of AC limits its use in high-temperature environments. To solve this problem, researchers have begun to consider developing modification methods for AC with lower combustion risks or studying methods to improve the safety of AC.

When selecting different AC modification methods, not only the safety factors of the adsorption should be taken into account as more and more fire accidents occurred in VOCs adsorption, but also the adsorption performance and economy of the adsorption should not be neglected [11,12]. The mainstream modification methods for AC include surface physical structure modification, surface chemical property modification, and electrochemical modification [13,14]. Li et al. [15] studied the thermal effect of spontaneous combustion in the preparation of coal-based activated carbon (CBAC). Li et al. [16]. modified the AC with the 4-amino-1,2-butanediol solution to study the physicochemical properties and natural properties of the AC before and after modification, and the results showed that the modified AC showed a lower natural trend. Therefore, how to modify AC to improve its flammable stability on the premise of adsorption efficiency has become the main goal of current research. However, the problems of the uncertain relation of AC structure and VOCs with fire tendency, the influencing factors, and secondary pollution are still unclearly. With thermal safety issues are much more serious with the increasing usage of AC nowadays, besides ensuring adsorption efficiency, researches on how to improve the thermal stability of AC is of great significance [17–20].

In recent years, carbon nanomaterials have been widely used to modify polymers to enhance their stability [21,22]. Among them, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new type of carbon nanomaterial with quasi-spherical morphology and nanoscale feature sizes [23,24]. Thanks to their high stability, easy functionalization of the surface, and friendly environment of raw materials, fluorescent CQDs have shown potential for impregnation modification applications [25,26]. Waheed et al. [27] synthesized N-CQDs by a solid-state reaction method, and it exhibited high thermal stability even under harsh thermal conditions. Navidfar et al. [28] studied the effect of CNT/CQDs on the properties of PU foams, and the results showed that CNT/CQDs could enhance the thermal stability of PU foams. Khose et al. [29] improved the functions of graphene CQDs. With the increasing of CQDs, the thermal stability can be significantly improved, effectively improving the ignition time of graphite. Research shows that silicon has high stability and excellent flame-retardant performance [30,31]. Li et al. [32] improved the flame-retardant performance of cotton fabric without changing its mechanical properties by preparing a phosphorus-nitrogen-silicon flame-retardant coating through the synergistic action of phosphorus, nitrogen, and silicon.

Silicon-based materials is a kind of typical thermostability materials [33–35]. It can enhance the heat-resistance capacity of the material when added as an additive. In this research, ethyl silicate, which has excellent thermal stability, and green materials of glycerol and betaine were chosen to prepare the Si dropped CQDs (Si-CQDs) firstly and then composited on AC to improve the thermal stability of AC through the synergistic action of CQDs and AC. Meanwhile, the rich surface functional groups on CQDs can optimize the chemical structure and pore structure on the surface of AC. This improvement will ensure sufficient active reaction sites for adsorption efficiency, and improve the combustibility and fire hazard of AC in VOCs adsorption. In the study, the physic-chemical properties of Si-dopped CQDs modified AC (AC/Si-CQDs) were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET), Fluorescence detection, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and X-ray diffraction (XRD). The influencing factors of fire hazard in VOCs adsorption by AC including the adsorption concentration of VOCs (toluene as the model VOC), the structure, particle sizes of AC, the dosages CQDs, and ambient moisture content were all investigated systematically. The thermal analysis research in adsorption, the thermal stability, self-ignition point, and absorbed heat release were carried out to further explore the mechanism the fire hazard of VOCs adsorption by AC.

AC was obtained from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Glycerol (C3H8O3, 99.0 %, Aladdin), Betaine (C5H11NO2, 99.0 %, Aladdin), and Tetraethyl orthosilicate ((C2H5O)4Si, 99.0 %, Aladdin) were provided by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Toluene was provided by Tianjin Yong da Chemical Reagent Co., (Tianjin, China). Ltd. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All materials and reagents are analytical reagents that can be used directly without further purification.

The size and distribution of CQDs were taken by transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEM-2100, Hitachi, Japan) under 200 kV acceleration voltage. The optimal emission wavelength of CQDs was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan) with a 150 W lamp as the excitation source. Study of the relative intensity of light absorption by CQDs using UV-6100S UV-Vis spectroscopy (Metash Instruments Co., Ltd., China). Cold-field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Hitachi S-4800, Hitachi, Japan) was used to characterize the topography of AC and AC/Si-CQDs. The specific surface area and pore volume of the AC and AC/Si-CQDs were tested by a fully automatic specific surface and porosity analyzer (BET) (Micromeritics 3Flex, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained using an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with AlKAlpha (hv = 1340 eV) under standard lens mode (Thermo Fischer, Waltham, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were characterized in the frequency range of 4000–400 cm−1 (Bruker Tensor 27, Germany). X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was taken using XRD-7000 (Shimadzu, Japan). The thermal stability of the AC before and after modification was tested by a synchronous thermal analyzer (STA 449 F5, Netzsch, Germany). Approximately 10 mg of the sample was tested in a nitrogen atmosphere and heated from 50 °C to 1000 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C·min−1. The self-ignition point of AC was measured under standard mode (target temperature is 400 °C and the heating rate is 0.5 °C/min) with a solid self-ignition point tester (HWP22–10E, Hangzhou Yangge Technology Co., Ltd., China). The heat change during the toluene adsorption process of AC was recorded using a micro-reaction calorimeter (THT-μRC, UK).

2.76 g of glycerol and 1.17 g of betaine were mixed in a flask, heated and stirred at 80 °C for 3 hours. Then, 0.65 g of tetraethyl orthosilicate was added to the container and heated for another two hours to obtain a homogenous transparent liquid. This liquid was then placed in an electric furnace and continuously stirred until it formed a black solid product. Place the product in a muffle furnace and heat at 300 °C for 3 hours. After grinding, the Si-CQDs powder can be obtained. The prepared Si-CQDs were dissolved in anhydrous ethanol and ultrasonically oscillated for 2 hours. After filtration with a 0.22 μm filter, the Si-CQDs solution was obtained. In the modified AC experiments, the AC was completely submerged in different concentrations of Si-CQDs solutions (mass fractions of 0.5 %, 1 %, 1.5 %, 2 %, and 2.5 %, respectively) for 24 h at room temperature. After impregnation, the Si-CQDs-loaded AC was transferred to a muffle furnace at a temperature of 300 ℃ and roasted for 3 h. After the roasting, the AC was naturally cooled to room temperature in the furnace, and the final products of AC loaded with different amounts of CQDs were AC/CQDs-0.5 wt%, AC/CQDs-1wt%, AC/CQDs-1.5 wt%, AC/CQDs-2wt%, and AC/CQDs-2.5 wt%, respectively ( Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spontaneous combustion properties of AC and AC/Si-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The spontaneous ignition point test experiment was performed according to GB/T 20450–2006. The instrument was set to the standard mode. Particle size, oxidation degree, and moisture were selected as variables to determine the spontaneous ignition point of AC, and the influence of various factors on it was analyzed. The adsorption device shown in Scheme 2 was constructed by μRC, a certain amount of toluene gas was introduced, the AC was evenly placed in the adsorption bottle, the Erlenmeyer flask containing NaOH was connected to the adsorption box, the tail gas was absorbed, and the AC after adsorption of toluene was weighed. To study the adsorption efficiency of AC and the influence of spontaneous ignition point before and after adsorption of toluene.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal analysis of AC and AC/Si-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the thermodynamic tests experiment, 10 mg of different AC powders were placed in a crucible separately, and the temperature was set by a synchronous thermal analyzer. The thermal stability of AC modified by different amounts of CQDs was analyzed by using temperature as the abscissa and sample weight as the ordinate.

Through the synchronous thermal analyzer, thermal weight loss analysis was carried out on the AC and the modified AC respectively. The instrument analyzed the thermal stability by testing the change in the quality of the sample before and after, and the heat change measured during heating was converted into a heat flow line. Finally, TG, DSC, and DTG curves were obtained from the experiment.

A microcalorimeter (μRC) was used to study the heat change in the process of toluene adsorption before and after the modification of AC. 0.3 g of AC before and after modification was taken to detect the thermal changes of different AC when absorbing 100 ppm toluene at 25 °C, 45 °C, 65 °C, and 110 °C.

The morphology and size characteristics of Si-CQDs can be observed through a transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fig. 1(a) shows that Si-CQDs have primary round shapes and are evenly distributed. The size distribution curve of Si-CQDs shown in Fig. 1(b) shows that the size of Si-CQDs is in the range of 1.0–10.0 nm, with a Gaussian-fitted average size of 2.9 nm.

XPS was used to explore the chemical bonding and elemental content of Si-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the full scan XPS results show that the main peaks are located at 103 eV, 285 eV, and 532 eV, which correspond to Si2p, C1s, and O1s respectively. This indicates that the elemental composition of Si-CQDs consists of C, O, and Si, with the contents of C, O, and Si being 72.71 %, 24.83 %, and 2.47 % respectively.

The three peaks in the C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) located at 284.61 eV, 285.45 eV, and 288.57 eV, are attributed to C-C/CC, C-O, and CO groups respectively. The majority of the deconvolved peaks in the O1s spectrum at 531.1 eV and 533.18 eV (Fig. 2c) correspond to the C-O-C/C-OH and CO groups respectively. Meanwhile, the peaks at 133.73 eV and 134.43 eV in the Si2p spectrum (Fig. 2d) are attributed to Si-O/SiO2 and Si-C groups respectively. Therefore, Therefore, XPS spectroscopy confirms the presence of stable SiO2 on the surface of Si-CQDs.

To further observe the material surface morphology, TEM and SEM were employed. With the different magnification of the modified TEM image of AC/Si-CQDs ( Fig. 3ab), it can be seen that spherical CQDs are present on the surface of AC, which indicates that the CQDs were successfully loaded on AC. The loading distribution of Si-CQDs on AC was relatively uniform. Compared with the original AC (Fig. 3c), the roughness of the carbon surface significantly increases in Fig. 3d, and more pores appear on the surface of the modified AC. The micropore surface area and the micropore volume have significantly increased compared to the original AC. In the impregnation, the CQDs solution penetrates into the interior of AC through capillary action driven by physical surface tension, and the alkaline solution corrodes the large pore of AC to micropore structure [36,37]. The carbon skeleton structure of AC is correspondingly changed during impregnation. At the same time, more carbon nanotube structures [38] are formed in AC, which significantly increases the specific surface area and carbon nanotube volume of the AC. The obvious roughed surface morphology and increased volume of AC would greatly improve the ability of AC to adsorb toluene.

For the detailed surface area and micropore volume of Si-CQDs modified AC, it can be seen from the results of BET ( Table 1). The specific surface area of increased by 86.9146 m2·g−1, the total pore volume increased by 0.0972 cm3·g−1, and the micropore volume increased by 0.1254 cm3·g−1 compared with the original AC, and the pore size was significantly improved. The increase of the specific surface area and micropore volume of the modified AC can significantly improve its adsorption capacity for toluene. The iodine value adsorption of AC and AC/Si-CQDs was carried out, and the specific experimental parameters and formulas used were shown in Formula (1) of auxiliary materials, and the iodine adsorption data were summarized in Table S1.

In the subsequent thermal activation treatment, the AC undergoes a chemical reaction with CQDs. The infrared spectra of the AC before and after modification are shown in Figs. 4. As can be seen from the black line in the figure, the characteristic peak of the original AC at 1078 cm−1 is attributed to the contraction vibration of C-O. The strong absorption peaks at 1652 cm−1 and 3398 cm−1 represent the expansion and contraction vibrations of CO and -OH bonds, respectively, indicating the presence of quinone and phenol groups, which give the AC a certain redox property. The vibrational absorption peak at 554 cm−1 of Si-CQDs-modified AC is the characteristic peak of the Si-O bond, and the vibrational absorption peak at 770 cm−1 is the characteristic peak of the Si-C bond. There is an obvious stretching vibration absorption at 1081 cm−1, which is the stretching vibration absorption peak of the Si-O-Si bond. A strong absorption peak appears at the wavelength of 3446 cm−1, which is the stretching vibration region of the -OH group. Compared with the original hydroxyl peak, it is significantly enhanced, indicating that a large number of hydroxyl groups and silicon dioxide microcrystals have been introduced on the surface of the AC, and the acidic functional groups have decreased. XRD plots of AC and AC/Si-CQDs (Fig. S2) demonstrate that in the modification by AC/Si-CQDs, the SiO2 begin to exist on the surface of AC, and the crystalline graphite are also increased. In this process, the AC was functionalized with the chemical groups of CQDs. At the same time, the physical pores structure also changed by CO2 activation at high temperature resulting lager surface area of AC. Moreover, the reaction between hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on AC and Si-CQDs may even promote the rearrangement of the surrounding carbon atoms to form a microporous structure. In all, the modification of AC with CQDs by impregnation and thermal activation is a kind of physics and chemistry coexist.

The thermal stability and thermal decomposition properties of AC and AC/Si-CQDs in air were studied by thermal analyzer. As can be seen from the Fig. 5, the AC undergoes a water evaporation phase at 97 ℃, resulting in a weight loss. However, the weight of AC/Si-CQDs in the water evaporation and desorption stages is small, which is due to the increase of the specific surface area of the modified AC, the pore size structure is richer, and the water is easier to evaporate. The initial decomposition temperature of AC was 216 ℃, and the initial decomposition temperature of AC/Si-CQDs was 425 ℃, indicating that AC/Si-CQDs had good thermal stability. In addition, the amount of carbon residue corresponding to the AC and AC/Si-CQDs at 800 ℃ increased from 2.2 wt% to 8.8 wt%, which may be due to the formation of a dense carbon layer on the surface of the AC, which effectively blocked the contact between the AC and the gas. Therefore, Si-CQDs can effectively improve the thermal stability of AC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fire resistance of AC/Si-CQDs in toluene adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: To reduce the risk of spontaneous combustion of AC, it is important to increase the auto-ignition point of AC during the modification by Si-CQDs, which can effectively reduce the occurrences of fires. The results of Fig. 6 indicate that as the amount of Si-CQDs increases, the auto-ignition point of AC would also increase. The spontaneous combustion temperature of unmodified AC is 319.3 °C. When the additional amount of Si-CQDs reaches 2.5 wt%, the auto-ignition temperature of AC becomes 379 °C, an increase of 59.7 °C after modification. However, when the addition of Si-CQD was more than 1.5 %, the growth of auto-ignition point would increase slowly, not as quick as before. So, the optimized addition was determined as 1.5 %. Therefore, as the amount of Si-CQDs increases, the higher the auto-ignition temperature of the AC. In previous studies, researchers have proven that Si can effectively enhance the thermal stability of AC [33–35]. In this study, it can be attributed to the presence of organosilicon on the surface of the CQDs. It can absorb heat and decompose to generate SiO2, forming a dense and stable silicon-containing carbon layer on the surface of the AC. This layer can prevent the dissipation of combustible gases produced by thermal decomposition, and can also serve as a heat and oxygen barrier, thereby increasing the auto-ignition point of the AC.

Figs. 7a and 7b showed the TGA and DTG curves of unmodified AC samples under a helium atmosphere, with heating rates of 5 °C, 10 °C, 15 °C, 20 °C, and 25 °C. Table 2 lists some key data. According to the TGA and DTG curves, the weight loss trend of unmodified AC at different heating rates is about the same. the initial weight loss rate temperature (T 0) increases from 393.79 °C to 457.49 °C, and the maximum mass loss rate temperature (T max) increases from 509.94 °C to 629.49 °C. The results indicate that the T 0 and T max of the unmodified AC increase as the heating rate increases, and the starting decomposition time of the AC also extends with the increase of the heating rate. This is because the thermal conductivity of AC powder is small. During the heating process, the weak thermal conductivity results in a slower internal heating rate of the AC, thus pushing the occurrence of the oxidation reaction of the AC in the system to the high-temperature region, and the release of heat is also delayed.

Figs. 7c and 7d were the TGA and DTG curves of the AC/Si-CQDs under a helium atmosphere, with heating rates of 5 °C, 10 °C, 15 °C, 20 °C, and 25 °C. The trend of the curves under different heating rates is roughly the same. The higher the heating rate, the higher the T 0 and T max, with T 0 increasing from 459.19 °C to 500.48 °C, and T max from 524 °C to 617.61 °C. The results show that compared with the unmodified AC, T 0 of the AC/Si-CQDs increases by 65.4 °C, 55.68 °C, 44.46 °C, 40.95 °C, and 42.99 °C, respectively. Si-CQDs can significantly increase the initial decomposition temperature of AC, thus greatly improving the fault tolerance of fire occurrence.

Apparent activation energy is one of the key parameters to study the oxidative combustion process. The higher the activation energy, the more difficult it is for AC to be oxidized, and vice versa. Using the Flynn-Wall-Ozawa (FWO) integration method, formula (1) is used to calculate the apparent activation energy E of AC and AC/Si-CQDs, with T max as the horizontal coordinate and lg(β) as the vertical coordinate, for linear fitting.

FWO formula [39]:
(1)
lg
(
β ) = lg
A E
R ⋅ g ( α )
− 2.315 − 0.4567
E
R
⋅
1
T
In the formula: R —— is the ideal gas constant, 8.314 J·mol−1·K−1;
E —— is the activation energy, J∙mol−1;
T —— is the peak temperature, K;
α —— is the conversion degree;
A —— is the pre-exponential factor, 1∙s−1;
β —— is the heating rate, K∙min−1.

There is a linear relationship between lg(β) and T −1. As shown in Fig. 8a, the fitting line is Y= -1752.518X + 4.196. The slope of the line obtained, −0.4567(E/R) = -1752.518, and R2 is 0.97939, from which we get E = 31.904 KJ∙mol−1. From the line intercept, ln (AE/Rg(a))-2.315 = 4.196, we can obtain the pre-exponential factor A= 175 s−1. As illustrated in Fig. 8b, the fitting line is Y= -3843.80X + 9.60. The slope of the line obtained is −0.4567(E/R) = -3843.80. From this, we can calculate E = 69.97 KJ∙mol−1. From the line intercept, ln (AE/Rg(a))-2.315 = 9.60, we can get the pre-exponential factor A= 1.8 × 104 s−1. The results of the apparent activation energy calculation of the AC/Si-CQDs are summarized in Table 3. The results show that the activation energy of AC/Si-CQDs is 69.97 KJ∙mol−1. Compared with the AC, the activation energy is significantly increased by 38.06 KJ∙mol−1, meaning more heat is needed for the oxidation reaction of AC/Si-CQDs, and the thermal stability is improved. This is because the AC/Si-CQDs have more crystalline areas on the surface. With stronger connections between molecules, it makes the structure of AC more stable, and requires more heat to disrupt the crystal structure of AC. At the same time, silicon oxides can also inhibit the thermal decomposition process of organic functional groups on the surface of AC. Therefore, the spontaneous combustion tendency of AC/Si-CQDs is significantly reduced, and the thermal stability is enhanced.

In the VOCs adsorption, the combustion accident of AC is a chain reaction. Firstly, the oxygen would be physically adsorbed in AC, and then, the oxidation reaction would release the reaction heat, thus resulting in increased temperature and intensified thermal movement of oxygen molecules. It would further enhance the heat release, making the internal temperature of AC rise correspondingly. At last, the temperature of AC reached the spontaneous combustion temperature [40,41]. When the system is under spontaneous combustion conditions with poor ventilation, AC will spontaneously combust. Therefore, the effects of different factors (such as particle size and moisture) on the spontaneous ignition point of AC were investigated.

The results of the spontaneous ignition point of AC and AC/Si-CQDs under different particle sizes and moisture content are shown in Figs. S3 and S4. As can be seen from Fig. S3, the spontaneous ignition point of AC/Si-CQDs is higher than that of the AC, and the spontaneous ignition temperature increases with the increase of particle size. Fig. S4 shows that the spontaneous ignition point of AC/Si-CQDs increases first and then decreases and then increases, and there is a minimum point of spontaneous ignition temperature, and the evaporation of water will cause pore channels or fissures to form inside the AC to facilitate the entry of oxygen. However, when there is too much water, the evaporation of water will absorb a large amount of heat, reducing the possibility of spontaneous combustion of AC The results showed that the spontaneous combustion performance of AC and AC/Si-CQDs was related to different treatment conditions. The adsorption capacity of toluene was summarized from the mass change before and after adsorption of AC and AC/Si-CQDs and was summarized in Table 4. The adsorption capacity of AC to toluene was 33.10 mg∙g−1, and the adsorption capacity of AC/Si-CQDs to toluene was 57.95 mg∙g−1, and the adsorption capacity increased by 24.85 mg∙g−1. The adsorption efficiency has been significantly improved. Further study of the spontaneous combustion characteristics of AC and AC/Si-CQDs after adsorption of toluene, the specific data are summarized in Table 5, and the spontaneous ignition point of AC/Si-CQDs after adsorption of toluene is 335.4 °C, which is 48.8 °C higher than that of AC. The results showed that the risk of spontaneous combustion after the adsorption of toluene by AC/Si-CQDs was lower than that after the adsorption of toluene by the AC, and the spontaneous combustion of the material was more likely to occur after the adsorption of toluene.

Besides spontaneous combustion, the heat accumulation in the toluene adsorption by AC and AC/Si-CQDs was also studied. The toluene adsorption experiment at different temperatures (25 °C, 45 °C, 65 °C,) was also carried out as a comparison. (Fig. S5). Fig. 9a shows the heat flow curve of toluene adsorption by AC. At various temperatures, as the temperature increases, the thermal power gradually increases, reaching a peak before balancing out. After a period, it drops quickly and then stabilizes. The lower the temperature, the lower the maximum thermal power and the shorter the time required to reach equilibrium. The area below the curve represents the energy released. According to calculations, at 25 °C, adsorption releases 131.02 J of heat, at 45°C adsorption releases 470.73 J of heat, at 65 °C adsorption releases 719.9 J of heat, and at 110 °C, adsorption releases 844.6 J of heat. Fig. 9b shows the heat flow curve of toluene adsorption by AC/Si-CQDs. At various temperatures, the trend in the heat flow curve of AC/Si-CQDs adsorption is similar to that of unmodified AC. As the temperature rises, the thermal power gradually increases, peaking before maintaining balance for a period, and then rapidly dropping and stabilizing. At 25 °C, the thermal power quickly drops after reaching the peak, without a balance phase, because the amount of toluene adsorbed by AC/Si-CQDs at 25 °C is small, due to the lower temperature. The area under the curve represents the heat of adsorption. According to calculations, at 25 °C, adsorption releases 165.83 J of heat, at 45 °C adsorption releases 483.62 J of heat, at 65 °C adsorption releases 478.7 J of heat, and at 110 °C, adsorption releases 948.9 J of heat. However, the heat released at 65 °C is less than that released at 45 °C. This suggests that the stability of AC/Si-CQDs at 65 °C is higher than at 45 °C.

We compared the role of Si-CQDs in promoting the thermal stability of AC with existing silica-based flame retardants, as shown in Table 6. Silicone-based flame retardants (silica, silicate, siloxane) were selected for comparison with Si-CQDs. Silicone-based flame retardants improve thermal stability by creating a physical barrier on the surface of the material to inhibit heat transfer and flammable gas escape. As a new type of CQDs, Si-CQDs contain functional groups such as Si-O/SiO2/Si-C on the surface, which affect the crystallization behavior and thermal decomposition pathway of materials through strong interaction with material molecules, and improve the thermal stability of materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of interaction between Si-CQDs and AC
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction mechanism between Si-CQDs and AC is shown in Fig. 10. With an average particle size of 2.9 nm, Si-CQDs can selectively fill the oversized mesopores or macropores in activated carbon to form a more uniform microporous structure. This filling reduces the non-uniformity of the pore size distribution and reduces the risk of pore collapse at high temperatures. In addition, the Si-O bond on the surface of Si-CQDs and the hydroxyl group on the surface of the activated carbon can form Si-O-C, and this chemical bonding will make the bond between Si-CQDs and activated carbon stronger, which will significantly improve the stability and performance of the composites. At the same time, the Si-O bond on the surface of Si-CQDs can also interact with the -COOH on the surface of activated carbon, which can increase the adsorption stability of Si-CQDs on the surface of activated carbon, so that Si-CQDs are more evenly distributed on the surface of activated carbon, to cover the active sites on the surface of activated carbon to a certain extent, and prevent the occurrence of thermal decomposition reaction.

This study synthesized a silicon-containing carbon quantum dot (Si-CQDs) modifier using betaine, glycerol, and tetraethyl orthosilicate and applied it to activated carbon (AC) for toluene adsorption and thermal stability analysis. The thermal properties were evaluated using Thermogravimetric analysis (TG) and micro-reaction calorimetry (μRC). The Si-CQDs modification significantly improved the thermal stability of the AC. Compared to the unmodified variant, the heat release during the toluene adsorption process was notably decreased in the modified AC. The self-ignition and initial decomposition temperatures of the modified AC increased by 59.7 ℃ and 65.4 ℃ respectively, and the activation energy increased by 38.07 kJ·mol−1, indicating greater resistance to ignition. The modification also enhanced the adsorption performance of the AC. The Si-CQDs encouraged the formation of more Si oxides on the activated carbon's surface, leading to stronger intermolecular forces and improved adsorption capacity. These oxides also have antioxidant properties, further enhancing the thermal stability of the AC and reducing its tendency to self-ignite. Si-CQDs modification can potentially widen the application of AC in gas adsorption for its enhanced fire resistance and adsorption performance.

Xiaoyu Huang: Visualization, Supervision. Xiaoyun Liu: Visualization, Investigation. Qing Li: Visualization, Formal analysis. Liang Han: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Hongwei Ren: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Zhang JinFeng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Zhaoxiong Huang: Visualization. Luyu Jia: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All the authors agree to submit the paper and publish it in this journal. Once the above paper is published in the journal, the authors agree to automatically transfer the copyright of the paper to the editorial department, including the rights of electronic publishing, multimedia publishing, online publishing and other forms of publishing. The authors solemnly promise that the paper is the original paper. All or part of the content has never been published in any other journals in any form. There is no problem of repeated submissions. There is no plagiarism or plagiarism. It does not include any violation of laws and regulations and content that infringes on the rights and interests of others. Once found that this article involves the above issues, the editorial department has the right to reject the manuscript in order to maintain the scientific ethics and normal publishing order, and the right to make real names for the paper and related authors in the field of education and scientific research and the scope of brother journals. The notice criticizes and has the right to notify the relevant units to impose serious administrative penalties on the main authors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (E2022208046), National Science Foundation of China (52004080).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2025.117114.
